# Mitochondria

The mitochondrion is a membrane-bound organelle found in the cells of most
eukaryotes. Mitochondria are often called the powerhouse of the cell because
they generate most of the cell's supply of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which
is used as a source of chemical energy.

Mitochondria have an outer membrane and a highly folded inner membrane. The
folds of the inner membrane are called cristae, and they increase the surface
area available for chemical reactions. The space inside the inner membrane is
called the matrix.

Mitochondria contain their own small genome, a circular DNA molecule called
mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA), which is inherited almost exclusively from the
mother. The endosymbiotic theory, advanced by Lynn Margulis in 1967, proposes
that mitochondria originated as free-living bacteria that were engulfed by an
ancestral eukaryotic cell.
